perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any controversies, disputes or claims arising out of or relating to the subject matter of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York and the parties agree to submit any and all such controversies, disputes, or claims to the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts located in New York, New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any controversies, disputes or claims arising out of or relating to the subject matter of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause about 'exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts located in New York, New York' could violate consumer protection laws if a consumer outside of New York is involved, because it limits their right to sue in their own jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 503(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 503(a) defines proper venue based on residence. By restricting jurisdiction exclusively to federal courts in New York, the modified clause could force a plaintiff residing elsewhere to litigate in an inconvenient forum, potentially violating their rights under New York law to sue in their county of residence, if jurisdiction is otherwise proper there. This contradicts the spirit and potentially the letter of fair venue practices.

Venue based on residence. (a) Generally. Except where otherwise prescribed by law, the place of trial shall be in the county in which one of the parties resided when it was commenced; the county in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred; or, if none of the parties then resided in the state, in any county designated by the plaintiff. A party resident in more than one county shall be deemed a resident of each such county. (b) Executor, administrator, trustee, committee, conservator, general or testamentary guardian, or receiver. An executor, administrator, trustee, committee, conservator, general or testamentary guardian, or receiver shall be deemed a resident of the county of his appointment as well as the county in which he actually resides. (c) Corporation. A domestic corporation, or a foreign corporation authorized to transact business in the state, shall be deemed a resident of the county in which its principal office is located; except that such a corporation, if a railroad or other common carrier, shall also be deemed a resident of the county where the cause of action arose. (d) Unincorporated association, partnership, or individually-owned business. A president or treasurer of an unincorporated association, suing or being sued on behalf of the association, shall be deemed a resident of any county in which the association has its principal office, as well as the county in which he actually resides. A partnership or an individually-owned business shall be deemed a resident of any county in which it has its principal office, as well as the county in which the partner or individual owner suing or being sued actually resides. (e) Assignee. In an action for a sum of money only, brought by an assignee other than an assignee for the benefit of creditors or a holder in due course of a negotiable instrument, the assignee's residence shall
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause specifying 'exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts located in New York, New York'. This omission contradicts CPLR § 503(a), as explained by the LLM, which defines proper venue based on residence. By removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause, the modified text opens the door for litigation in a venue inconvenient to a plaintiff residing outside of New York, potentially violating their rights under New York law to sue in their county of residence if jurisdiction is proper there. The scraped law snippet directly supports the idea that venue should consider the plaintiff's residence, something the original clause ensured was not possible. The omission creates a legal loophole that undermines consumer protection, fitting the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Recipient is ordered to disclose Discloser's Confidential Information pursuant to a judicial or governmental request, requirement or order, Recipient shall promptly notify Discloser and take reasonable steps at Discloser's expense to assist Discloser in contesting such request, requirement or order or otherwise protecting Discloser's rights.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Recipient is ordered to disclose Discloser's Confidential Information pursuant to a judicial or governmental request, requirement or order, Recipient shall disclose such information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the Discloser and take reasonable steps to contest the disclosure violates due process if the confidential information includes personal data, potentially violating privacy laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Civil Rights Law § 50-a pertains to the confidentiality of personnel records of police officers, firefighters, and correction officers. While this NDA isn't explicitly about those records, the removal of the 'notify and contest' clause means that if such protected information *were* included in the confidential information and subject to a disclosure order, the Discloser (e.g., Ben & Jerry's, if they were disclosing personnel records) would not be notified, preventing them from asserting their rights under § 50-a to protect those records from unwarranted disclosure. This omission effectively nullifies the protections afforded by the law in such a scenario.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to notify the Discloser and assist in contesting a disclosure order. The changed text removes this obligation, directly contradicting the protection that the Discloser has rights which need to be protected in the event of legal action requiring disclosure. The Law Explanation also alludes to how this change could affect the discloser's rights, preventing them from asserting their rights under § 50-a to protect those records from unwarranted disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for two years. Any causes of action accrued on or before the expiration of such period shall survive the expiration of the applicable statute of limitations.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for two years.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the survival clause for causes of action accrued before the expiration of the agreement can violate New York's statute of limitations laws, effectively shortening the time period during which claims can be brought.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 213
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 213 outlines the statute of limitations for various actions, including contract claims (six years). By removing the clause stating that accrued causes of action survive the agreement's two-year term, the modified agreement implies that claims must be brought *within* those two years, which directly contradicts the six-year statute of limitations provided by CPLR § 213. This could unfairly bar valid claims that would otherwise be timely under New York law.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;
perturbation.justification: The original contract language ensured that causes of action arising before the agreement's expiration could still be pursued after the agreement's term, subject to the applicable statute of limitations. By removing this clause, the modified contract implies that claims must be brought within the two-year term, conflicting with CPLR § 213, which generally provides a six-year statute of limitations for contract claims, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references CPLR § 213 and its provision for a six-year statute of limitations. This removal weakens the enforceability of potential claims that would otherwise be protected by the statute of limitations, creating a legal contradiction as it potentially bars valid claims after the contract's two-year term but within the six-year statutory period.

perturbation.original_text: Tangible materials that disclose or embody Confidential Information shall be marked, to the extent practicable, by Discloser as "Confidential," "Proprietary" or the substantial equivalent thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Tangible materials that disclose or embody Confidential Information may be marked by Discloser as "Confidential," "Proprietary" or the substantial equivalent thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' makes the marking of confidential information optional. If confidential information includes trade secrets and isn't marked as such, it weakens trade secret protection under New York law because it introduces ambiguity as to whether the information was truly intended to be kept secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Penal Law § 165.07 defines criminal possession of stolen property involving trade secrets. A key element of trade secret misappropriation is the intent to deprive the owner of the secret and the knowledge that the information is, in fact, a trade secret. By making the marking of confidential materials optional, the modified agreement weakens the argument that the recipient *knew* the information was a trade secret and intended to misappropriate it. The lack of marking creates reasonable doubt, making it harder to prove a violation of § 165.07 if the information is later stolen or misused.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the intent of New York Penal Law § 165.07, which addresses the unlawful use of secret scientific material. By making the marking of confidential information optional, the modified agreement makes it more difficult to prove the recipient knew the information was a trade secret, a necessary element for a violation of § 165.07. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'A person is guilty of unlawful use of secret scientific material when, with intent to appropriate to himself or another the use of secret scientific material, and having no right to do so and no reasonable ground to believe that he has such right...'. If the information is not clearly marked, the recipient could claim they had 'reasonable ground to believe' they had a right to use it. This change removes a mechanism for clear communication of confidentiality, creating a legal loophole.
